bytes.topic: Container Fundamentals — images vs containers, layers, copy-on-write filesystem
bytes.title: Helm Image Values — Layers, Digests, and Copy-on-Write at Replica Scale
bytes.body:
Every Helm chart deployment ultimately references a container image — a read-only stack of filesystem layers. Helm's canonical values.yaml pattern separates image.repository, image.tag, and image.pullPolicy deliberately: each controls how Kubernetes resolves and caches those layers on the node.

imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent tells the kubelet to skip the registry when image layers are already cached — critical for fast pod restarts and air-gapped clusters. Always forces a manifest digest check on every start, catching updated :latest tags at the cost of registry round-trips.

Prefer digest pinning over mutable tags in production charts. image.digest: sha256:abc123... produces an immutable layer reference. Override it with --set image.digest=sha256:... on helm upgrade and helm history will track the exact layer stack per revision — something a floating tag like :v1.2 can never guarantee.

The copy-on-write filesystem matters most when replicaCount is high. All 20 pods on a node share the same read-only image layers — only each pod's thin writable CoW layer differs. Helm's rolling update knobs, maxSurge and maxUnavailable in the deployment template, directly control how many new CoW layers Kubernetes provisions in parallel during an upgrade cycle.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Pin by digest in values.yaml — tag left empty to avoid ambiguity
# image.repository: ghcr.io/myorg/api
# image.tag: ""
# image.digest: "sha256:a1b2c3..."
# image.pullPolicy: IfNotPresent

# Upgrade with new digest — immutable, fully tracked by helm history
helm upgrade my-app ./chart \
  --set image.digest=sha256:a1b2c3d4e5f67890abcdef1234567890abcdef12 \
  --set replicaCount=20

# Confirm all pods resolved to the same layer stack (CoW: one set per node)
kubectl get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/name=my-app \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.spec.containers[0].image}{"\n"}{end}'

bytes.topic: Dockerfile Best Practices — layer caching, multi-stage builds, minimal base images
bytes.title: Helm Deployments Are Faster When Your Dockerfile Isn't Lazy
bytes.body:
Helm deployments live and die by image pull time and rollout stability. Optimizing your Dockerfile directly reduces `helm upgrade` rollout duration and prevents the silent failures that plague production releases.

Layer caching is your first lever: copy dependency manifests (`go.mod`, `package.json`, `requirements.txt`) before source files. Docker rebuilds only layers below the first changed layer — so a source-only change skips the expensive dependency install on every CI pipeline that feeds `helm upgrade`.

Multi-stage builds eliminate build toolchain from your runtime image. A Go binary built from `golang:1.22-alpine` drops to ~8MB in `distroless/static:nonroot` — roughly 30x smaller than the builder stage. Kubernetes nodes pull this image far faster, cutting time-to-Ready for pods during Helm rollouts with `--atomic`.

Pin images in Helm values by digest, not just tag — tags are mutable and cause `helm upgrade` to silently deploy different code across environments. Capturing the SHA256 digest post-build and passing it via `--set image.digest` makes your `helm history` a true, reproducible audit trail. Combine with `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` to leverage node-level layer cache on re-deploys.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Build and capture immutable digest — never trust a mutable tag
docker build -t myregistry/api:v1.2.3 .
DIGEST=$(docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' myregistry/api:v1.2.3 | cut -d@ -f2)

# Helm upgrade pinned by digest for reproducible, auditable rollouts
helm upgrade my-app ./charts/my-app \
  --set image.repository=myregistry/api \
  --set image.tag=v1.2.3 \
  --set image.digest="${DIGEST}" \
  --set image.pullPolicy=IfNotPresent \
  --atomic \
  --timeout 5m

bytes.topic: Image Security — CVE scanning, non-root user, distroless images
bytes.title: Helm Image Security — Digest Pinning, Non-Root Contexts, and Distroless Defaults
bytes.body:
Mutable tags like `latest` are a supply-chain liability. Pin images by digest in your chart's `values.yaml` so every `helm upgrade` is reproducible and auditable. In your Deployment template, prefer `{{ .Values.image.repository }}@{{ .Values.image.digest }}` over tag interpolation.

For non-root enforcement, set `securityContext` defaults directly in the chart template — don't leave it to the operator. `runAsNonRoot: true`, `runAsUser: 65534` (nobody), and `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` should be chart-level defaults, overridable only via explicit values.

Distroless images (e.g. `gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12`) eliminate the shell attack surface entirely — pair them with `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` and drop all capabilities via `capabilities.drop: [ALL]`.

For CVE scanning in CI, use a Helm pre-upgrade hook Job that runs Trivy against the resolved image before rollout proceeds. If the Job fails, Helm aborts the release — no manual gate needed. Set `helm.sh/hook-delete-policy: before-hook-creation` to keep the namespace clean across upgrades.
bytes.code_snippet:
# values.yaml
image:
  repository: gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12
  digest: sha256:a1b2c3d4e5f6...  # no mutable tag

securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  runAsUser: 65534
  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
  capabilities:
    drop: ["ALL"]

# templates/pre-upgrade-scan.yaml
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: {{ include "mychart.fullname" . }}-cve-scan
  annotations:
    helm.sh/hook: pre-upgrade
    helm.sh/hook-weight: "-5"
    helm.sh/hook-delete-policy: before-hook-creation
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
        - name: trivy
          image: aquasec/trivy:latest
          args:
            - image
            - --exit-code=1
            - --severity=CRITICAL
            - "{{ .Values.image.repository }}@{{ .Values.image.digest }}"

bytes.topic: Kubernetes Architecture — control plane vs worker nodes, etcd, API server
bytes.title: Helm and Kubernetes Architecture — How helm install Touches the Control Plane
bytes.body:
Every `helm install` or `helm upgrade` call goes exclusively through the kube-apiserver — Helm never talks to etcd, the scheduler, or kubelets directly. Helm 3 stores release state as Kubernetes Secrets (type `helm.sh/release.v1`) in the target namespace; those secrets are serialized, gzipped, and base64-encoded before the API server persists them to etcd. This means etcd is your release history's source of truth — not Helm's local cache.

On the worker node side, Helm only authors manifests. Once `helm install` POSTs resources to the API server, the controller manager reconciles them and the scheduler assigns pods to nodes. Helm's `--wait` flag polls the API server's watch stream until Deployments report `Available`, giving you a synchronization point without coupling Helm to any individual node.

If the API server is unreachable, every Helm command fails — even `helm list`, which reads release secrets from the cluster, not a local file. Understanding this flow matters when debugging: a failed `helm upgrade` with a partial rollout means etcd holds the new release Secret but the controller manager may still be reconciling worker-node pods.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Inspect Helm release secrets stored in etcd (via API server)
kubectl get secrets -n my-namespace \
  -l owner=helm,status=deployed \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}'

# Decode a release secret to inspect the stored manifest
kubectl get secret sh.helm.release.v1.my-release.v3 \
  -n my-namespace \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.release}' \
  | base64 -d | base64 -d | gunzip | jq '.manifest'

# helm upgrade with API-server watch synchronization
helm upgrade my-release ./chart \
  --wait \
  --timeout 5m \
  --atomic

bytes.topic: Pods & Deployments — pod lifecycle, replica sets, deployment strategies
bytes.title: Helm — Controlling Pod Lifecycle and Deployment Strategies with values.yaml
bytes.body:
Helm doesn't manage ReplicaSets or Deployments directly — it templates Kubernetes manifests, so your deployment strategy lives in `values.yaml` and gets stamped into the `spec.strategy` block of a `Deployment` resource. The two critical strategies are `RollingUpdate` (default) and `Recreate`. With `RollingUpdate`, Helm-rendered charts respect `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable` to control how many pods are cycled simultaneously — misconfiguring these causes downtime during `helm upgrade`. Set `maxSurge: 1` and `maxUnavailable: 0` for zero-downtime deploys.

Helm's `--atomic` flag couples lifecycle to the upgrade: if the new ReplicaSet's pods don't reach `Ready` within `--timeout`, Helm auto-rolls back. Pod readiness is gated by `readinessProbe` — if your chart omits this, `--atomic` has nothing to wait on and upgrades appear to succeed when pods are still crashing. Always define `livenessProbe` and `readinessProbe` in your chart templates. Use `helm rollback <release> <revision>` to revert to any prior Helm-managed Deployment state tracked in the release history.
bytes.code_snippet:
# values.yaml
replicaCount: 3

strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxSurge: 1
    maxUnavailable: 0

readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10

livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 15
  periodSeconds: 20

# Deploy with atomic rollback on failure
# helm upgrade --install my-app ./chart --atomic --timeout 3m

bytes.topic: Services & Networking — ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer, cluster DNS
bytes.title: Helm Service Types — ClusterIP vs NodePort vs LoadBalancer and Cluster DNS
bytes.body: When you define a Service in a Helm chart, the `service.type` value in `values.yaml` dictates reachability. `ClusterIP` (default) exposes the Service only inside the cluster — other Pods reach it via DNS at `<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local`. This is the right default for internal microservices; never expose internals with a broader type. `NodePort` binds a port (30000–32767) on every Node's IP, useful for dev clusters without a cloud LB — but it's fragile in production. `LoadBalancer` provisions a cloud load balancer (AWS ALB, Azure LB) automatically; pair it with `service.annotations` in your values to pass cloud-specific config (e.g. `service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type`). In Helm charts, template your Service type cleanly: gate `nodePort` and `loadBalancerIP` fields with conditionals so they only render when relevant. Cluster DNS is automatic — once deployed, `helm install myapp ./chart -n payments` makes `myapp-payments.payments.svc.cluster.local` resolvable immediately. Use `ClusterIP` by default; override via `--set service.type=LoadBalancer` at install time for environment-specific promotion.

bytes.topic: ConfigMaps & Secrets — externalizing config, secrets encryption at rest
bytes.title: Helm ConfigMaps & Secrets — Externalizing Config and Encrypting at Rest
bytes.body:
Helm separates config from code via `values.yaml`, but the real power comes from templating ConfigMaps and Secrets directly from those values. Define your app config in `values.yaml`, then render it into a ConfigMap using `{{ .Values.config | toYaml | indent 4 }}`. For Secrets, base64 encoding is automatic when you use the `Secret` kind — Helm's `b64enc` function handles encoding inline: `{{ .Values.db.password | b64enc | quote }}`.

For encryption at rest, the standard tool is `helm-secrets` backed by SOPS (Secrets OPerationS). Encrypt a values file with `helm secrets enc secrets.yaml`, committing the ciphertext safely to Git. At deploy time, `helm secrets upgrade` decrypts on the fly using a KMS key (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or Azure Key Vault) — the plaintext never touches disk. Alternatively, use the `secrets://` driver with the vals plugin for dynamic resolution at render time. Avoid committing raw `Secret` manifests or unencrypted values files — treat any file referenced by `-f` as a potential leak surface.
bytes.code_snippet:
# values.yaml
db:
  host: postgres.internal
  password: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:abc123==,type:str]

# templates/secret.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: {{ include "myapp.fullname" . }}-db
type: Opaque
data:
  password: {{ .Values.db.password | b64enc | quote }}

# Encrypt and deploy
helm secrets enc values/secrets.yaml
helm secrets upgrade myapp ./chart -f values/secrets.yaml

bytes.topic: Resource Management — requests vs limits, QoS classes, avoiding OOMKill
bytes.title: Helm — Resource Requests vs Limits, QoS Classes, and Surviving OOMKill
bytes.body:
Every Helm chart you deploy lands in one of three Kubernetes QoS classes based on how you configure resources in your values.yaml — and that class determines who dies first under node pressure.

BestEffort (no requests or limits set) gets evicted immediately. Burstable (requests < limits, or only one set) gets a middle seat. Guaranteed (requests == limits for both CPU and memory) is the last to go.

In your chart templates, always expose both fields via values so operators can tune per-environment. A common mistake: setting only limits without requests — Kubernetes defaults requests to the limit value, giving you Guaranteed class unintentionally in dev but Burstable in prod when someone overrides limits only.

For memory, OOMKill happens when a container exceeds its limit — no grace period, just SIGKILL. Set memory limits conservatively: use kubectl top pods after load testing, then add a 20–30% buffer. CPU throttling is recoverable; memory OOMKill is not. Use Helm's .Values pattern to enforce sane defaults while allowing per-release overrides via --set or a separate values file.
bytes.code_snippet:
# values.yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"

---
# templates/deployment.yaml
containers:
  - name: {{ .Chart.Name }}
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: {{ .Values.resources.requests.cpu | quote }}
        memory: {{ .Values.resources.requests.memory | quote }}
      limits:
        cpu: {{ .Values.resources.limits.cpu | quote }}
        memory: {{ .Values.resources.limits.memory | quote }}

bytes.topic: Health Checks — liveness vs readiness vs startup probes
bytes.title: Helm Health Checks — Wiring Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes in Your Chart
bytes.body:
Helm doesn't abstract Kubernetes probes — it just templates them. The power is in parameterizing probe configs so operators can tune them per-environment without forking your chart.

All three probes live under `spec.containers[].livenessProbe`, `readinessProbe`, and `startupProbe` in your Deployment template. A common pattern is to expose them as `values.yaml` keys: `livenessProbe`, `readinessProbe`, `startupProbe` — each an object that gets injected verbatim using `toYaml | nindent`. This lets users override the entire probe block without touching the template.

Use `startupProbe` for slow-starting containers (JVMs, model-loading services) — it gates the other two until the app is up. Set `failureThreshold * periodSeconds` to your worst-case cold-start budget. Once it passes, `livenessProbe` takes over to restart deadlocked pods and `readinessProbe` controls Service endpoint membership.

Avoid hardcoding `initialDelaySeconds` in the template — it's a smell. Prefer `startupProbe` with a generous threshold and zero `initialDelaySeconds` on liveness/readiness. Expose `livenessProbe.httpGet.path` and port as separate values so the chart works across apps with different health endpoints.

bytes.topic: Helm — chart structure, values files, templating, release management
bytes.title: Helm — Mastering Values Merging and Template Overrides Across Environments
bytes.body:
Helm resolves values through a deterministic merge chain: chart defaults (values.yaml) < umbrella chart < -f flag files (left to right) < --set flags. Later sources win. This means you can layer environment configs cleanly: a base values.yaml defines production-safe defaults, then values.staging.yaml overrides only what differs.

Templates live in templates/ and use Go text/template with Helm's Sprig extensions. The .Values object exposes your merged values tree; .Release.Name and .Chart.Name are built-in objects injecting release metadata. Use named templates in _helpers.tpl with {{- define "app.labels" -}} blocks and invoke them via {{- include "app.labels" . | nindent 4 }}—prefer include over template because include captures output for pipeline chaining.

Release state is stored in Kubernetes Secrets (default) as base64-encoded gzipped JSON. helm history <release> surfaces the full revision log; helm rollback <release> <revision> atomically restores the prior manifest set. Use helm diff upgrade (from the helm-diff plugin) before every apply in CI to catch unintended drift before it hits the cluster.

bytes.topic: Storage in K8s — PersistentVolumes, storage classes, StatefulSets
bytes.title: Helm StatefulSets — Wiring PersistentVolumes and Storage Classes the Right Way
bytes.body:
StatefulSets in Helm require `volumeClaimTemplates` — not `volumes` — to get stable, per-pod PVCs. Unlike Deployments, each StatefulSet pod gets its own PVC (e.g., `data-myapp-0`, `data-myapp-1`) that survives pod restarts and is never garbage-collected on `helm uninstall` by default. This is intentional: data outlives the release.

Parameterize the `storageClassName` through `values.yaml` so operators can swap between `standard`, `premium-ssd`, or cloud-native classes (`gp3`, `managed-premium`) without touching the template. Always expose `storage` size as a value too — resizing a bound PVC is painful and often requires manual intervention.

Critical Helm gotcha: `helm upgrade` cannot modify `volumeClaimTemplates` — it's an immutable field on the StatefulSet spec. Any change triggers a failed upgrade. The only safe path is delete-and-recreate, which means pre-upgrade PVC backup is your responsibility. Use `helm upgrade --force` only if data loss is acceptable. For production, treat storage config changes as migrations, not upgrades.
bytes.code_snippet:
# values.yaml
storage:
  className: gp3
  size: 20Gi

# templates/statefulset.yaml
volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: {{ .Values.storage.className }}
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: {{ .Values.storage.size }}

bytes.topic: Autoscaling — HPA, VPA, KEDA for event-driven scaling
bytes.title: Helm + Kubernetes Autoscaling — HPA, VPA, and KEDA in One Chart
bytes.body:
Helm charts ship autoscaling primitives out of the box, but wiring HPA, VPA, and KEDA together requires deliberate values design. Most official charts expose a `autoscaling.enabled` toggle that renders an HPA manifest — but VPA and KEDA require separate CRD-backed resources you must template yourself.

For HPA, the standard pattern uses `spec.metrics` with `ContainerResource` type (Kubernetes 1.27+) rather than the legacy `Resource` type, giving per-container granularity. Drop it under `templates/hpa.yaml` and gate it with `{{- if .Values.autoscaling.hpa.enabled }}`.

For KEDA, install the `kedacore/keda` Helm chart as a dependency in `Chart.yaml`, then define a `ScaledObject` template referencing your Deployment. KEDA's `ScaledObject` replaces the HPA entirely — never run both simultaneously on the same workload, or you'll get conflicting replica counts.

VPA is advisory-only by default (`updateMode: Off`) — useful for right-sizing `requests` without disrupting pods. Add it as a separate template and enable independently. Use `helm template` locally to validate all three render correctly before `helm upgrade --install`.

bytes.topic: RBAC & Namespaces — multi-tenancy, least privilege in cluster
bytes.title: Helm & Kubernetes RBAC — Scoping Releases to Namespaces with Least Privilege
bytes.body:
Helm respects Kubernetes RBAC boundaries entirely — it operates using the credentials of whoever runs `helm install`. In multi-tenant clusters, this means you must scope each tenant's service account to its own namespace and bind only the roles it needs.

Create a dedicated ServiceAccount per tenant, then bind a Role (not ClusterRole) limited to the resources Helm actually touches: Secrets (for release state), ConfigMaps, Deployments, and Services. Helm 3 stores release metadata as Secrets in the target namespace by default (`--storage-driver=secret`), so the service account needs `get/list/create/update/delete` on Secrets in that namespace only — not cluster-wide.

Pass the scoped kubeconfig or service account token via `KUBECONFIG` or `helm --kube-context`. Use `helm install --namespace tenant-a --create-namespace` to keep releases isolated. Validate the blast radius with `helm list --all-namespaces` to confirm no cross-namespace leakage. For CI pipelines, generate short-lived tokens with `kubectl create token` rather than static kubeconfig files.
bytes.code_snippet:
# rbac.yaml — scoped Role + binding for a Helm service account
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: helm-tenant-a
  namespace: tenant-a
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: helm-deployer
  namespace: tenant-a
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["secrets", "configmaps", "services"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "delete"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: helm-deployer-binding
  namespace: tenant-a
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: helm-tenant-a
    namespace: tenant-a
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: helm-deployer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

bytes.topic: Network Policies — pod-to-pod traffic rules, ingress controllers
bytes.title: Helm Network Policies — Locking Down Pod-to-Pod Traffic Without Hardcoding IPs
bytes.body: Helm's `.Values` system lets you template NetworkPolicy manifests so the same chart works across namespaces without manual IP edits. The key is using `podSelector` with `matchLabels` that mirror your chart's own label convention — typically `app.kubernetes.io/name: {{ include "mychart.name" . }}`. For ingress controllers, you need a separate `namespaceSelector` rule targeting the namespace where your ingress controller lives (e.g., `ingress-nginx`). Without it, your controller can't reach pods even if external traffic hits the Service. Pair `podSelector: {}` with `namespaceSelector` to allow cross-namespace traffic only from that controller namespace. Use `helm template` locally to render the policy and verify selectors match actual pod labels before applying — label mismatches silently drop traffic. Gate the entire NetworkPolicy block behind a `Values.networkPolicy.enabled` flag so teams can opt out in dev clusters where CNI plugins don't enforce policies. This keeps your chart portable across environments without forking templates.
bytes.code_snippet:
{{- if .Values.networkPolicy.enabled }}
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: {{ include "mychart.fullname" . }}
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      {{- include "mychart.selectorLabels" . | nindent 6 }}
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app.kubernetes.io/name: ingress-nginx
{{- end }}

bytes.topic: Rolling Updates & Rollbacks — zero-downtime deployments, rollback strategy
bytes.title: Helm Rolling Updates & Rollbacks — Zero-Downtime Deployments Done Right
bytes.body:
Helm delegates rolling updates to Kubernetes, but your chart values control the strategy. Set `strategy.type: RollingUpdate` with `maxUnavailable: 0` and `maxSurge: 1` in your deployment spec to guarantee zero downtime — no pod goes down until a healthy replacement is running. Helm's `--atomic` flag ties the release to this: if the rollout fails, it automatically rolls back and marks the release as failed.

Track history with `helm history <release>` — each `helm upgrade` increments the revision. Roll back instantly with `helm rollback <release> <revision>`, which re-applies the exact chart + values from that revision. Limit stored history via `--history-max` to avoid unbounded secrets accumulation in your cluster.

For canary-style deploys, combine Helm with separate releases (e.g. `myapp-canary`) or weight-based ingress annotations — Helm itself has no native traffic-splitting primitive. Always pair upgrades with `--timeout` and `--wait` so CI/CD pipelines block until pods are truly ready, not just scheduled.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Upgrade with zero-downtime guarantees and auto-rollback
helm upgrade myapp ./chart \
  --atomic \
  --timeout 5m \
  --wait \
  --history-max 10 \
  -f values.prod.yaml

# Inspect revision history
helm history myapp

# Roll back to a specific revision
helm rollback myapp 3 --wait

# Corresponding deployment strategy in values.yaml:
# deployment:
#   strategy:
#     type: RollingUpdate
#     rollingUpdate:
#       maxUnavailable: 0
#       maxSurge: 1

bytes.topic: Podman — rootless containers, daemonless architecture, Docker compatibility
bytes.title: Helm OCI Pipelines with Podman — Rootless Builds, No Daemon Required
bytes.body:
Helm CI/CD pipelines traditionally depend on a Docker daemon — a root-level process that's a liability in shared build environments. Podman eliminates this with a daemonless, rootless architecture: each `podman build` forks directly from the calling process, running in a user namespace with no persistent socket to babysit.

The Docker compatibility layer is the key unlock for Helm workflows. Podman exposes a systemd socket at `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/podman/podman.sock` implementing the Docker HTTP API verbatim. Pointing `DOCKER_HOST` at it means `helm registry login`, `helm push`, and your pre-deploy image builds all work without pipeline rewrites.

For Helm OCI workflows specifically, `helm push` targets any OCI-compliant registry — the same endpoint Podman pushes application images to. No privilege escalation, no `/var/lib/docker` contention across parallel build agents. Rootless image layers land in `~/.local/share/containers`, making agent-level isolation trivial.

In Kubernetes environments using CRI-O or containerd (common in Helm-deployed clusters), Podman's daemonless model aligns naturally — both speak OCI Image Spec directly, so images built with Podman carry no Docker-specific metadata that could trip up your `helm install` rollout.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Activate Podman's Docker-compatible socket (no sudo)
systemctl --user enable --now podman.socket
export DOCKER_HOST=unix://$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/podman/podman.sock

# Rootless image build — feeds into Helm chart values
podman build -t myregistry.io/myapp:1.3.0 .
podman push myregistry.io/myapp:1.3.0

# Helm OCI chart workflow — same registry, no daemon
helm package ./charts/myapp
helm registry login myregistry.io
helm push myapp-1.3.0.tgz oci://myregistry.io/charts

# Deploy with pinned image tag
helm upgrade --install myapp oci://myregistry.io/charts/myapp \
  --version 1.3.0 \
  --set image.tag=1.3.0

bytes.topic: Local Development — Docker Compose, kind, minikube, dev vs prod parity
bytes.title: Helm Local Dev — Matching prod parity with kind and values overrides
bytes.body:
Running `helm install` against a kind or minikube cluster exposes a common trap: your chart works locally but breaks in prod because you're shipping different values. The fix is layered values files — keep `values.yaml` as your prod baseline and override with `values.local.yaml` for local dev.

Use `helm install myapp ./chart -f values.yaml -f values.local.yaml` — Helm merges right-to-left, so the local file wins on conflicts. In `values.local.yaml`, disable PodDisruptionBudgets, set `replicaCount: 1`, swap in `NodePort` instead of `LoadBalancer`, and point image tags to `localhost:5000/myapp:dev` (your local registry). For kind, pre-load images with `kind load docker-image` to skip the registry entirely.

Crucially, never commit `values.local.yaml` — add it to `.gitignore`. Use `helm template myapp ./chart -f values.yaml -f values.local.yaml | kubectl diff -f -` to diff what would change before any apply. This gives you a reproducible local loop without drifting from the chart your CI deploys.
bytes.code_snippet:
# values.local.yaml — never committed
replicaCount: 1
image:
  repository: localhost:5000/myapp
  tag: dev
  pullPolicy: Never
service:
  type: NodePort
podDisruptionBudget:
  enabled: false
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 50m
    memory: 64Mi

# Load image into kind, then install with layered values
kind load docker-image localhost:5000/myapp:dev --name dev-cluster
helm upgrade --install myapp ./chart -f values.yaml -f values.local.yaml

bytes.topic: Observability in K8s — logs, metrics, traces from pods
bytes.title: Helm — Wiring Full Observability into K8s Pods with One Chart
bytes.body:
Helm's dependency system lets you co-deploy the full observability stack alongside your app in a single `helm install`. Define Loki, Prometheus, and Tempo as `dependencies` in `Chart.yaml`, then wire them to your pods via `values.yaml` — no manual kubectl piping required.

For logs, enable Loki's `promtail` scrape config to tail pod stdout/stderr by label. For metrics, annotate pods through your chart's `deployment.yaml` template with `prometheus.io/scrape: 'true'` and `prometheus.io/port`; Prometheus picks these up via its Helm-deployed `ServiceMonitor` CRD. For traces, inject the OpenTelemetry Collector as a sidecar using a Helm sub-chart, pointing `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` to Tempo via an env var in your `values.yaml`.

The real leverage: override per-environment in `values-prod.yaml` — crank up scrape intervals, toggle trace sampling rates, or disable Loki in local dev. One chart, three signal types, environment-aware without template duplication.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Chart.yaml
dependencies:
  - name: loki-stack
    version: "2.10.2"
    repository: "https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts"
  - name: kube-prometheus-stack
    version: "58.1.3"
    repository: "https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts"
  - name: tempo
    version: "1.7.2"
    repository: "https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts"

# values.yaml
loki-stack:
  promtail:
    enabled: true

kube-prometheus-stack:
  prometheus:
    prometheusSpec:
      serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues: false

tempo:
  tempo:
    reportingEnabled: false

# In your deployment template
annotations:
  prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
  prometheus.io/port: "{{ .Values.app.metricsPort }}"
